title: TCP Connections, Accept Loops, Half-Closes, Deadlines, and Failure · Execution Trace: Write Ambiguity Retry
summary: A raw server treats TCP as messages, leaks accepted connections, uses no deadlines, retries ambiguous writes, and cannot distinguish peer close, timeout, reset, or local shutdown. Planned artifact: Add and verify the write ambiguity retry increment in bounded TCP connection lifecycle state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Treat failed or timed-out writes as potentially partially observed and avoid blind replay of non-idempotent protocol effects.
Distinguish orderly EOF, CloseRead, CloseWrite, full close, reset, timeout, and local shutdown effects on protocol completeness.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Write Ambiguity Retry · Theory Write Ambiguity Retry
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 8860 in a municipal gateway receiving one-byte TCP fragments. Build deterministic Go evidence to classify connection, deadline, completion, and close state without inventing message boundaries; preserve this operating constraint: the maintained Go protocol stack remains the production transfer target; then force a short write and retain the exact transmitted prefix plus remaining count. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Write Ambiguity Retry contract for the supplied data.
